back: Use of organisms, cells or enzymes to make useful products and processes.
Traditional uses whole organisms and fermentation; modern biotechnology uses DNA-level tools such as recombinant DNA technology.
Genetic engineering and contamination-free bioprocess engineering.

front: Why does DNA move in an electric field?
back: Its phosphate backbone gives it a net negative charge.
It remains active after repeated high-temperature denaturation steps.
The sequence where replication begins in a cloning vector; it influences copy number.
Sticky ends have single-stranded overhangs; blunt ends do not.

front: Why is EcoRI called a molecular scissors?
back: It recognises a specific DNA sequence and cuts DNA at that site.
Joining DNA fragments, usually by DNA ligase sealing the sugar-phosphate backbone.
Uptake of foreign DNA by a host cell.
A host cell made able to take up foreign DNA.

front: What does CaCl2 do in transformation?
back: It helps make bacterial cells competent for plasmid uptake.
Disruption of a functional marker gene due to insertion of foreign DNA.
A selection idea where marker disruption changes colony colour, helping identify recombinants.
Recovery of a desired DNA fragment from agarose gel.
Denaturation, annealing and extension.
Introduces sterile air or oxygen bubbles into a bioreactor.
Separation, purification, formulation, quality control and release.
Proinsulin has A, B and C peptide; mature insulin has A and B chains after C-peptide removal.
Bt protoxin becomes active in the alkaline insect gut and damages target pest midgut cells.
Gene silencing through degradation or blocking of matching mRNA.
ADA deficiency.
Detection based on antigen-antibody interaction.
Evaluates biosafety and validity of GM organism research and release in India.
